title: Naive Bayes for Text: The Spam Filter
concept: Naive Bayes treats text as a bag of words, counting word frequencies in spam vs. normal emails to calculate probabilities.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the benefit of the 'naive' independence assumption in Naive Bayes?

options.text: It simplifies the mathematical calculations, making the model very fast to train
It guarantees the model will never make a mistake
It allows features to influence each other's probabilities

exercises.explain: Exactly! The assumption of independence makes the calculations much simpler and faster.

cards.title: Sorting your mail
cards.body: How does your email app know that "WIN A FREE TRIP NOW!" is spam, while "Hey, can we grab lunch?" is safe? It does this by checking the words in the email. And Naive Bayes is one of the most famous algorithms for sorting them out.

cards.title: Bag of Words
cards.body: Naive Bayes doesn't actually read or understand sentences. It treats an email as a bag of words. It ignores grammar and word order, and simply counts how often individual words appear in "Spam" emails versus "Normal" (or non-spam) emails.

cards.title: Two word jars
cards.body: Imagine you have two jars filled with words. The Spam Jar has lots of words like "free", "winner", and "urgent". The Normal Jar has words like "meeting", "project", and "lunch". When a new email arrives, we see which jar is more likely to have generated that mix of words.

cards.title: Calculating the word 'Free'
cards.body: If the word "free" appears in 80 out of 100 spam emails, its spam probability is 80%. If it only appears in 2 out of 100 normal emails, its normal probability is 2%. When the model sees "free" in a new email, it uses these numbers to push the prediction toward "Spam".

cards.title: Grammar doesn't matter
cards.body: You might think the model understands the meaning of the email. In fact, to Naive Bayes, "Win a free prize" and "Prize free a win" look exactly the same! Because it assumes words are independent (the naive assumption), it just multiplies their individual probabilities together.

exercises.prompt: How does a Naive Bayes spam filter view an email?

options.text: As a 'bag of words' where grammar is ignored and word frequencies are counted
As a complete story with deep grammatical meaning and context
As a grid of pixels to be measured with distances
As a single continuous number representing time

exercises.explain: Yes! Naive Bayes treats text as a 'bag of words', ignoring grammar and counting word frequencies.

exercises.prompt: True or false: To a Naive Bayes model, the phrase 'buy lunch' and the phrase 'lunch buy' are treated as completely different inputs.
exercises.explain: False! Since it treats text as a 'bag of words' and ignores word order, both phrases contain the exact same words and are treated identically.

exercises.sentence: Naive Bayes calculates the probability of an email being spam by multiplying the probabilities of its individual ___.
exercises.explain: The model calculates the probability of each word appearing in spam vs. normal emails, and multiplies them together.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents how a Naive Bayes spam filter decides if an email containing the words 'win' and 'cash' is spam?

options.text: It multiplies the probability of 'win' appearing in spam by the probability of 'cash' appearing in spam, and compares it to the normal email probabilities.
It checks the distance on a coordinate map between the words 'win' and 'cash'.
It reads the email sentence by sentence to check for polite grammar.

exercises.explain: Exactly! It calculates the combined probability by multiplying the individual word probabilities, assuming they are independent.

exercises.prompt: Suppose the word 'urgent' appears in 90% of spam emails and only 1% of normal emails. If a new email arrives with the word 'urgent', how does this affect the Naive Bayes calculation?

options.text: It significantly increases the calculated probability that the email is spam.
It has no effect because the model only counts the total number of letters.
It forces the model to immediately classify the email as normal.
It resets all other word probabilities to zero.

exercises.explain: Spot on! Since 'urgent' is highly associated with spam, its presence increases the overall calculated spam probability. Next, let's look at the strengths and weaknesses of this method!
